food_name: Kueh Tutu (Steamed Rice Flour Cone Cakes with Coconut or Peanut Filling)

local_names.hindi: कुएह तुतु (नारियल / मूंगफली भरावन के साथ उबले चावल के आटे के केक)

aliases: 米粉蒸糕 (Mǐ Fěn Zhēng Gāo)
कुएह तुतु (नारियल / मूंगफली भरावन के साथ उबले चावल के आटे के केक)

chinese: 米粉蒸糕 (Mǐ Fěn Zhēng Gāo)
hindi: कुएह तुतु (नारियल / मूंगफली भरावन के साथ उबले चावल के आटे के केक)

food_subgroup: Steamed rice flour cone cakes with sweet coconut or peanut filling; traditional Singaporean hawker snack

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Kueh Tutu as a prepared Singaporean hawker snack. Closest parent would be steamed rice cake (FOODON:03301710) but no exact match is available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed rice flour cake with sweet filling (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for Kueh Tutu)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0KDA used as closest parent for steamed rice flour cake. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this traditional Singaporean preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Kueh Tutu as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Kueh Tutu is 3 pieces (~90g), freshly steamed and served in a small banana or pandan leaf wrapper at a Singapore hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Kueh Tutu — HPB Singapore Foodo / Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kueh Tutu. Values represent the composite dish (rice flour shell with coconut or peanut filling) as sold at Singapore hawker stalls. Per-100g values are as reported; serving size of 90g (3 pieces) is consistent with HPB portion guidance.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Rice flour and steamed rice-based products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kueh Tutu in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy to steamed rice flour products (plain rice flour steamed cake GI ~65–70; partial gelatinisation during steaming moderates GI slightly relative to fully cooked white rice at GI ~72). Glycemic load per 90g serving = (65 × 47.97g available carbohydrate per 90g) / 100 = 31.2; rounded to 31.2. Note: GL recalculated as (65 × 53.3 × 0.90) / 100 = 31.2 for 90g serving. The fat content from coconut or peanut filling modestly attenuates postprandial glucose response.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~97mg per 90g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Kueh Tutu contains approximately 97mg sodium per 3-piece serving (90g), making it one of the lowest-sodium hawker snacks in Singapore; it is suitable for individuals managing hypertension and does not require sodium restriction adjustments when consumed as an occasional snack.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~65) and moderate glycemic load (~31 per 90g serving); significant carbohydrate content (~48g per 90g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 65 and a glycemic load of ~31 per 3-piece serving, Kueh Tutu should be consumed in controlled portions by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the fat from coconut or peanut filling modestly slows glucose absorption, but carbohydrate content (~48g per 90g) must be accounted for within the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~2.25g per 90g serving), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and very low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kueh Tutu has a favourable cardiovascular profile with zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (~2.25g per 90g serving), and very low sodium (~97mg per serving); the peanut filling variant provides modest MUFA, and the food is suitable as an occasional snack for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low sodium, no processed meat, low saturated fat, modest MUFA and vitamin E from peanut filling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kueh Tutu poses low inflammation risk at a standard 3-piece serving; the peanut filling variant provides modest MUFA (~2g) and vitamin E (~0.36mg), and both filling variants are low in pro-inflammatory nutrients at this serving size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kueh Tutu (土土糕) is one of Singapore's most iconic and rapidly vanishing traditional hawker snacks — small, white dome-shaped steamed cakes made from finely ground rice flour pressed into metal cone moulds over a live steamer, filled with either freshly shredded coconut mixed with sugar or ground roasted peanuts with sugar. The outer shell is soft, slightly chewy, and delicately fragrant from the rice flour, while the filling is sweet and moist (coconut) or nutty and crumbly (peanut). Served fresh in a small banana or pandan leaf wrapper, Kueh Tutu is a Peranakan and Hokkien Chinese heritage food with roots in Singapore's early hawker culture. The name 'tutu' is believed to derive from the Hokkien word for the wooden steamer sound (thôh-thôh) or from the vessel shape. As of 2026, only two live-steam Kueh Tutu stalls remain in Singapore — at Harbourfront MRT and Fort Canning Park. Nutritionally, a 3-piece serving (90g) provides approximately 270 kcal, 4.5g protein, 7.5g fat, 48g carbohydrate, and only ~97mg sodium — making it one of Singapore's lowest-sodium hawker snacks. GI is estimated at ~65 (medium), GL ~31 per serving. It is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. The coconut filling variant provides lauric acid (saturated fat ~2.25g per serving) while the peanut variant provides MUFA and vitamin E. Suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management; moderate caution for Type 2 Diabetes due to carbohydrate load. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo database.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singapore dishes). The food is a well-documented traditional Singaporean hawker snack with consistent preparation methods. GI is estimated by analogy to steamed rice flour products from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which slightly reduces confidence for the GI component to medium; however, overall record confidence remains high given the strong primary source for macronutrients and micronutrients.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA/PUFA split) for Kueh Tutu is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. Total fat (8.3g/100g) and saturated fat (2.5g/100g) are confirmed; remaining fat is unsaturated but the MUFA/PUFA split is not available. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this traditional steamed preparation is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of April 2026. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal-derived ingredients in standard preparation). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed negligible — no significant carotenoid or retinol sources). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — vegan food, no animal products). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no significant dietary vitamin D source). 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on steamed rice flour products in the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for Kueh Tutu exists in published literature as of April 2026. Glycemic load per 90g serving calculated as (65 × 47.97) / 100 = 31.2 (available carbohydrate = 53.3 × 0.90 = 47.97g). 4. Two portions are documented: 1 piece = 35g (coconut filling, HPB verified) and 3 pieces = 90g (standard serving, HPB verified). The default serving used for GL calculation is 90g (3 pieces). 5. FoodEx2 code A0KDA used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 or FoodOn code exists for Kueh Tutu as a specific prepared hawker food. 6. Allergen note: peanut filling variant contains peanuts (tree nut allergen); coconut filling variant is peanut-free. Both variants are gluten-free and dairy-free.
